item.title: Rabuka calls for sugar industry transformation
item.description: PRIME Minister Sitiveni Rabuka says Fiji’s sugar industry requires a fundamental transformation, with large-scale mechanised farming and modernised production systems needed to reverse declining output and secure the sector’s future. Speaking after a tour of the sugar belt, Mr Rabuka said harvesting was progressing well and mills were operating, but warned that current production levels remained b
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister Sitiveni Rabuka says Fiji’s sugar industry requires a fundamental transformation, with large-scale mechanised farming and modernised production systems needed to reverse declining output and secure the sector’s future.

Speaking after a tour of the sugar belt, Mr Rabuka said harvesting was progressing well and mills were operating, but warned that current production levels remained below what Fiji needed to meet domestic demand and export commitments.

Mr Rabuka said Fiji needed increased production from both farmers and mills to supply the local market and maintain its obligations to major export partners, including the European Union.

“Fiji needs more output from the farmers and from the mills to be able to satisfy our local market, as well as our international obligations with the markets of the European Union that is buying most of our exported sugar.”

Looking beyond the current season, he said long-term reform must focus on mechanisation. “The real solution should be large-scale farms, mechanised cultivation, and mechanised harvesting.”

He acknowledged challenges posed by Fiji’s land tenure system and expiring Agricultural Landlord and Tenant Act (ALTA) leases, saying future solutions would require cooperation between Government, iTaukei Land Trust Board and the sugar industry.

Mr Rabuka also raised concerns over ageing mill infrastructure, saying years of underinvestment had affected efficiency. “Some of those machines are just out of date. Nobody else is manufacturing those, so that we can get the parts.” Despite the challenges, he said modernisation remained achievable.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Island pays final tribute
item.description: THE chiefly island of Bau will tomorrow observe the vakataraisulu ceremony for the late Turaga na Taukei Naisogolaca, Ratu Epeli Nailatikau, as traditional mourning rites continue for the former president and speaker of Parliament. Proceedings will begin at 10am with a church service before a series of customary ceremonies held on the island.
item.content:encoded:
THE chiefly island of Bau will tomorrow observe the vakataraisulu ceremony for the late Turaga na Taukei Naisogolaca, Ratu Epeli Nailatikau, as traditional mourning rites continue for the former president and speaker of Parliament.

Proceedings will begin at 10am with a church service before a series of customary ceremonies held on the island.

A isevusevu and presentation of traditional gifts will be made by the Tui Nayau, Tui Lau and Sau ni Vanua o Lau, Ratu Tevita Uluilakeba Mara.

Tunitoga Aisea Jack Komaitai said the Roko Tui Dreketi Ro Teimumu Kepa is also expected to attend the ceremonies, although confirmation had yet to be received.

The day’s program will include the luva isulu ceremonies involving Ratu Epeli’s daughter, Adi Litia Cakobau Nailatikau, who will first visit the residence of the Turaga Vunivalu na Tui Kaba at Mataiwelagi before accompanying her mother, the Radi Naisogolaca and widow of Ratu Epeli, Adi Koila Nailatikau to visit the Roko Tui Bau at Naicobocobo.

The ceremonies will culminate with the veiqaravi vakavanua on the village green on Bau.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Bau preserves ancient customary obligations
item.description: THE chiefly island of Bau yesterday completed the traditional duki, one of the final customary obligations in honour of Ratu Epeli Nailatikau, with elders choosing to preserve the ceremony in its original form despite calls for changes. Held under overcast skies and intermittent rain, the ceremony drew the people of the Bau together in a display of unity as women entered the water to drive fish to
item.content:encoded:
THE chiefly island of Bau yesterday completed the traditional duki, one of the final customary obligations in honour of Ratu Epeli Nailatikau, with elders choosing to preserve the ceremony in its original form despite calls for changes.

Held under overcast skies and intermittent rain, the ceremony drew the people of the Bau together in a display of unity as women entered the water to drive fish towards waiting nets held by young men.

Turaga Masau Louis Toganivalu served as the ovisa ni duki, overseeing the traditional protocols and ensuring the ceremony was conducted according to customary practice.

As required under tradition, no one was permitted to leave until the duki had concluded.

The ceremony was led by Adi Asenaca Qalirea.

The last duki was held in 2016 during the customary rites for the late Roko Tui Bau and former vice-president of Fiji, Ratu Joni Madraiwiwi.

Speaking after the ceremony, Apenisa Seniloli of the Mataqali Tui Kaba said discussions had taken place over whether changes should be made to the duki, given the number of years since it was last performed.

However, he said it was ultimately agreed that the ceremony should be carried out according to ancient custom.

Mr Seniloli said the duki remained a powerful expression of the values of oneness and togetherness that have long defined the people of Bau.

Despite the wet weather, the community carried out the ceremony with determination, with women vigorously splashing the water to herd fish into the nets as part of the traditional practice.

Among those taking part was 26-year-old Sereana Vodovakatani of Lasakau Village, who participated in the duki for the first time.

She described it as a memorable experience that taught her the importance of teamwork and the value of fulfilling traditional obligations to the vanua.

The customary mourning rites for the late Turaga nai Taukei Naisogolaca will continue with the vakataraisulu ceremony, scheduled to begin at 10am tomorrow.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Diabetic foot surgery tops list
item.description: SEVENTY per cent of the country’s surgical workload is made up of diabetic foot surgery. Assistant Minister for Health Penioni Ravunawa revealed the statistic yesterday while speaking about the importance of a new minor operating theatre funded by the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.
item.content:encoded:
SEVENTY per cent of the country’s surgical workload is made up of diabetic foot surgery.

Assistant Minister for Health Penioni Ravunawa revealed the statistic yesterday while speaking about the importance of a new minor operating theatre funded by the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.

Mr Ravunawa said diabetic foot disease is one of the biggest reasons patients require surgery, making the additional operating facility an important step in improving patient care.

“We have over 220 village health workers all across the country,” he said.

“And their work is to educate the villagers, the people in the settlements and communities to take care of their own health.

“Meaning their diet choice, their physical exercise, their rest time, all that are important.”

However, Mr Ravunawa said Fijians continue to rely on herbal medicine and other faith-based beliefs before seeking medical treatment.

He said this often results in patients presenting to hospitals only after their diabetes-related complications have reached the stage of necessary amputation.

Mr Ravunawa said the new minor operating theatre will allow diabetic foot procedures to be carried out more quickly and closer to patients in the surgical ward.

“Collectively, these improvements represent a significant step forward in strengthening paediatric care and ensuring that every child receives safe, dignified and compassionate healthcare.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Bauleka turns challenges into strength
item.description: MISS Nadi Town Council Fifita Bauleka is not letting her personal challenges and difficult moments define her as she takes on the 2026 Nadi International Airport Bula Festival stage. She is excited to be part of this year’s carnival standing alongside nine other contestants vying for the 2026 crown.
item.content:encoded:
MISS Nadi Town Council Fifita Bauleka is not letting her personal challenges and difficult moments define her as she takes on the 2026 Nadi International Airport Bula Festival stage.

She is excited to be part of this year’s carnival standing alongside nine other contestants vying for the 2026 crown.

“Like many people, I have gone through personal challenges that have tested my confidence, self-worth, and humanity,” said Ms Bauleka.

“While I am not proud of every moment during those difficult periods, I do not believe those moments define who I am.

“My decision to enter the Bula Festival was never about being a role model or proving anything to anyone, it was about finding myself again, rebuilding my confidence and giving back to my community through a cause I believe in.”

Ms Bauleka thanked her supporters and close family members for their ongoing support during this important part of her life.

Meanwhile, the final private judging of the contestants will be held today.

The event will close on Saturday with a float procession at 1.30pm and the crowning of Miss Bula 2026 at Prince Charles Park from 7pm.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PEOPLE | High on music: Ben’s journey to turn passion into a dream
item.description: Benjamin Seniroqa still remembers the years when he and his bandmates would spend hours creating music videos before taking the stage at Traps Bar in Suva. What began as a creative hobby has since evolved into Only Idea Studios, a Fijian production company specialising in filmmaking, animation, visual effects and 360-degree videography, with clients reaching far beyond Fiji’s shores.
item.content:encoded:
Benjamin Seniroqa still remembers the years when he and his bandmates would spend hours creating music videos before taking the stage at Traps Bar in Suva.

What began as a creative hobby has since evolved into Only Idea Studios, a Fijian production company specialising in filmmaking, animation, visual effects and 360-degree videography, with clients reaching far beyond Fiji’s shores.

Originally from Moce in Lau with maternal links to Kadavu through his mother’s family, Ben, as he is commonly known, described himself first and foremost as a musician.

But alongside his lifelong love of music has always been an equally strong passion for visual storytelling.

Music has been part of his life since the age of seven, when he first played drums in church.

Growing up in a family surrounded by musicians, creativity came naturally.

His uncle, Johnny Ram, guitarist who has performed alongside icon Seru Serevi, introduced him and his siblings to music at an early age, while his cousins and younger brother all played instruments, creating an environment where performing became second nature.

Although he completed a Diploma in Networking through USP’s TAFE programme and built a career in information technology with Greymouse and later Telecom Fiji, the pull of the creative industries never disappeared.

After leaving the IT sector, he returned to full-time music, performing locally and overseas, including in Guam and Australia, before gradually expanding his interest in photography and videography into a business.

Only Idea Studios officially registered in 2019, although the team had already spent years producing music videos and experimenting with visual effects.

In many ways, he shared that the company grew directly out of the band, with several former members becoming part of the studio before eventually establishing successful careers and businesses of their own.

Today, the company has carved out a niche by combining 360-degree videography with advanced visual effects, attracting overseas clients seeking expertise rarely available elsewhere in the region.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Praise for joint effort
item.description: A partnership between the Government and the local community has delivered upgraded school facilities aimed at providing students with a safer and more conducive learning environment. Speaking at the commissioning ceremony of the Draiba Primary School hall in Suva yesterday, Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka said the project, valued at $58,048.66, was jointly funded through the Office of the Prime Mi
item.content:encoded:
A partnership between the Government and the local community has delivered upgraded school facilities aimed at providing students with a safer and more conducive learning environment.

Speaking at the commissioning ceremony of the Draiba Primary School hall in Suva yesterday, Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka said the project, valued at $58,048.66, was jointly funded through the Office of the Prime Minister’s Small Grant Scheme and the school community.

Mr Rabuka said the project reflected the true meaning of solesolevaki — working together for the common good.

“It is a very powerful demonstration of what can be done when the community and Government, or government agencies, help to build lasting indications of progress with our contribution to future progress,” Mr Rabuka said.

He said while the term solesolevaki had become widely used in recent years, the completed project was a practical example of the concept in action.

“This project is a very good demonstration of the solesolevaki spirit.”

Mr Rabuka said the commissioning reaffirmed the Government’s commitment to ensuring all children had access to quality education facilities, regardless of where they lived.

“Today’s commissioning reaffirms your Government’s commitment to ensuring that every child, regardless of whether they live in urban or rural and maritime areas of Fiji, has access to safe, modern and quality learning environments that will support their future potential.”

Mr Rabuka said investing in schools was an investment in the nation’s future and thanked everyone who contributed to making the project a reality.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: BACK IN HISTORY | Arya makes history
item.description: Suva teacher Urmilla Arya made history when she became the first woman elected to a top position in the Fiji Teachers Union in 1994. She had completed a course in Introduction to Language and Educational Project, Education and Social Development, Human Development, Educational Administration, Decision-Making and Educational Planning and Development at a teachers’ college.
item.content:encoded:
Suva teacher Urmilla Arya made history when she became the first woman elected to a top position in the Fiji Teachers Union in 1994.

She had completed a course in Introduction to Language and Educational Project, Education and Social Development, Human Development, Educational Administration, Decision-Making and Educational Planning and Development at a teachers’ college.

According to a report in The Fiji Times on Thursday May 12, 1994, her most challenging time in her career was at the Crippled Children’s School in Lautoka.

“It is very different here because I am working with children who have different needs,” Ms Arya said.

She spent more time going over their schoolwork and held multiple classes where she had to teach students of different levels at one time.

“It’s hard at this school where I learnt to work with handicapped children and experienced teaching multiple classes.”

She joined the FTU in 1983. From 1983 to 1989, she served as assistant secretary of the FTU Suva branch. In 1989, she was elected vice-chairperson of the FTU Central Division committee and later became chairperson of the FTU Women’s Wing. At the time, Ms Arya also noted a shift in the union.

“More women are speaking out. They are asking more questions. Women are more confident now and have come a long way.”

For her, the work came back to children.

“The development of children is high on my agenda, that is the reason I became a teacher,” she said.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
